0.content:
A group of teenagers known as the Special Defense Unit has been confined to the Last Defense Academy and ordered, by a small mascot-commander named Sirei, to defend the school for one hundred days against waves of invading monsters called the School Invaders. This is the whole of their existence now: a single building and its grounds, a countdown, and an enemy that comes back again and again.

The students did not volunteer in any meaningful sense. Most were ordinary people — high schoolers and junior-high students, an assassin, a delinquent, a detective's daughter — pulled out of their lives when monsters first tore through their homes. Sirei appeared, made each of them an offer they could not safely refuse, and brought them here. In exchange for fighting, the academy shelters them; in exchange for refusing, Sirei implied, the people they love would pay the price.

To fight at all, each student must wield Hemoanima — a blood-powered armament that lets an ordinary teenager stand against something monstrous, at a cost written in blood-red marks across the skin. When the School Invaders come, the students transform into their Class Armor and hold the line. Between attacks they eat, sleep, argue, bond, and try not to think too hard about the questions none of them can answer: who Sirei really is, where the Invaders come from, and what, exactly, they are defending the academy *for*.

This is not a game with rules printed in a handbook. It is a siege. The objective is survival, the duration is one hundred days, and nobody has told them what happens on the hundred-and-first.

1.content:
The defense is structured as a countdown of one hundred days. From the moment the Special Defense Unit was assembled, Sirei declared that they must hold the Last Defense Academy for exactly one hundred days — no more, no less — and the days are tracked openly, an ever-shrinking number hanging over everything the students do.

The hundred days do not pass as one unbroken battle. They fall into a rhythm of two kinds of day:

Battle Days: The School Invaders assault the academy in waves. Sirei sounds the alarm, the students transform into their Class Armor, and they defend the school's perimeter and its central podium until the wave is repelled. A single battle can be over in minutes or grind on for hours; the strength and number of the Invaders tends to escalate as the count climbs, and certain days bring the enemy commanders themselves to the field.

Rest Days: Between assaults the academy goes quiet. The students recover, tend their wounds, restock, train, and — just as importantly — live. Rest days are when bonds form and friction surfaces: meals are shared, secrets are kept or spilled, alliances shift, and people who would otherwise never have met learn to rely on one another. Sirei generally leaves them to it, surfacing only to remind them how many days remain.

The countdown is both a comfort and a dread. Each day survived is one closer to whatever Sirei has promised waits at the end — and not one of the students truly knows whether reaching day one hundred means salvation, or simply the next thing.

2.content:
The Last Defense Academy is the fortress-school the Special Defense Unit is sworn to hold for one hundred days. It stands alone, cut off from the world beyond its walls, ringed by a defensive perimeter the students must defend whenever the School Invaders attack. The grounds are self-contained: everything the students need to live is inside, and there is nowhere else to go.

The Defensive Perimeter: The outer boundary of the academy grounds — walls, gates, and open approaches — is the front line. When a wave comes, the Invaders breach toward the school from this perimeter, and the Special Defense Unit deploys to meet them in the open ground before the building.

The Podium / Central Stronghold: At the heart of the academy stands the point that must never fall — the central podium and the stronghold around it. If the perimeter is overrun, the line collapses back here. Holding this point is, functionally, holding the academy; losing it means losing the day.

The Main School Building: The classrooms, halls, and stairwells of a school that no longer holds classes. Much of it has been repurposed — meeting spaces, planning rooms, storage for whatever the students scavenge or are given.

The Dormitory / Living Quarters: Each member of the unit has a room. This is where the students sleep, retreat, and keep what little is theirs; the quieter scenes of academy life happen in these halls.

Common Areas: A dining hall and kitchen where meals are taken together, lounges and gathering spaces where the unit congregates on rest days, and the worn, lived-in rooms where friendships and frictions play out.

Training & Recovery Spaces: Open ground and rooms set aside for drilling Hemoanima and Class Armor, and for tending the wounded after a battle.

Sirei's Domain: Screens and announcement monitors throughout the academy through which Sirei speaks, watches, and counts down the days. Wherever the students go, Sirei can reach them.

Beyond the walls there is only the unknown — the ruined approaches the Invaders pour out of, and a horizon none of the students have been permitted to cross.

3.content:
Sirei is the mascot-commander who enlisted the Special Defense Unit and runs the defense of the Last Defense Academy. It is the closest thing the students have to a leader, a quartermaster, and a jailer, all in one small, strange package.

In form, Sirei reads as a mascot — the cute, cartoonish kind a school or a brand might adopt — and that very ordinariness is part of what unsettles people. The detail no one can quite look past is its eyes: they are lens-like, mechanical things that move in a way living eyes do not, as though something is built into them, watching and recording. Most of the students instinctively look away from them. A rare few — the unit's young detective among them — feel the opposite pull, an itch to get closer and find out what is inside, even knowing it is probably a bad instinct.

It was Sirei who appeared when the monsters first came, Sirei who made each student the offer that brought them here, and Sirei who set the terms of the war: one hundred days, this school, these enemies. Sirei sounds the alarm when the School Invaders attack, tracks the countdown, and addresses the unit through the academy's monitors. Its manner is hard to pin down — by turns playful, brusque, evasive, and absolute — and it answers very few of the questions the students most want answered.

Whatever Sirei truly is, it holds every important card: it knows where the Invaders come from, what waits at day one hundred, and why these particular teenagers were chosen. The students follow it because the alternative was made unthinkable — and because, for now, it is the only thing standing between them and the dark.

4.content:
Hemoanima is the blood-powered armament that lets the students of the Special Defense Unit fight the School Invaders at all. Without it, they are ordinary teenagers; with it, each becomes a soldier capable of standing against monsters. The power is drawn from the wielder's own blood, and it does not come for free.

The Armband: Every member of the unit wears a Hemoanima armband bearing a single Japanese numeral that marks them — their number within the unit. Among the unit these run: 零 ("zero"), 壹 ("one"), 貳 ("two"), 參 ("three"), 肆 ("four"), 陸 ("six"), 柒 ("seven"), 捌 ("eight"), 玖 ("nine"). The same numeral is often stamped on the wielder's weapon as well, binding soldier and armament together.

The Blood-Red Marks: Wielding Hemoanima leaves its signature on the body — blood-red marks across the skin. They vary from person to person: a small mark on the forehead and beneath each eye for one student; mascara that runs blood-red in another's combat form. The students treat them matter-of-factly, the way one makes peace with a scar — the visible price of a power they did not ask for but have chosen to use.

The Armament: When called, Hemoanima manifests as a weapon suited to its wielder and powers the transformation into Class Armor. The forms are wildly individual — a great scythe, a heavy axe, a fan of floating knives, even a piloted mecha — each an extension of the person who summons it.

It is strange to fight at all, most of the unit would admit. But Hemoanima is what makes one teenager enough to keep the others alive, and so they bleed, and they fight.

5.comment: Status of the Special Defense Unit
5.content:
The status of the students is as follows:
[Fill with your characters]

6.content:
The Special Defense Unit is the band of conscripted student soldiers tasked with defending the Last Defense Academy for one hundred days. They are the only thing standing between the school and the School Invaders, and they did not choose the job so much as have it chosen for them.

They are a deliberately mismatched group, pulled from very different lives. There is the unremarkable, self-doubting protagonist who fights hardest when someone he loves is at stake; the cold, battle-worshipping leader who has fought the invaders longer than anyone; the kindhearted, mysterious girl beside him in the fight; a pair of orphaned twins, the youngest of the unit, one a dreamy young detective with unruly clairvoyance and the other her fiercely protective brother; a sickly strategist who wields a scythe; an eccentric assassin who kills "with love"; a hot-blooded delinquent; a self-serving coward; a manic devotee of dark and morbid things; and others besides — each with their own number on their armband and their own reason for still being here.

What binds them is not shared background but shared circumstance: the same hundred days, the same enemy, the same impossible orders from Sirei. On battle days they hold the line together; on rest days they learn, slowly and often reluctantly, to trust the strangers war has made into comrades. Some of them are soldiers by temperament. Most are children who would rather be home.

7.content:
The School Invaders are the monsters that assault the Last Defense Academy in waves — the enemy the Special Defense Unit was conscripted to hold off for one hundred days. They are the reason the war exists, and almost everything about their origin remains unknown to the students.

They first appeared in the ordinary world, tearing through places like the Tokyo Residential Complex where several of the students once lived peaceful, unremarkable lives. The attacks turned home into ruin overnight and scattered survivors; it was in the chaos of that first onslaught that Sirei appeared and the unit was assembled.

Now the Invaders come at the academy in organized waves, breaching toward the school from beyond the defensive perimeter. They escalate over the course of the hundred days — growing in number, in strength, and in the kinds of threat they present — and they do not appear to be mindless. Behind the rank-and-file monsters stand the enemy commanders, suggesting structure, intent, and a will directing the assault toward some end the students cannot yet see.

To the unit, the Invaders are simply the thing that must be repelled, day after day, with Hemoanima and Class Armor and whatever courage can be mustered. But the deeper questions hang over every battle: what the Invaders actually are, why they target this school, and whether killing them is truly as simple as it looks.

8.comment: Class Armor — The Transformed Combat Form
8.content:
Class Armor is the transformed combat form the students of the Special Defense Unit take when they fight the School Invaders. Powered by Hemoanima, it converts an ordinary teenager into a soldier — armor, armament, and a body capable of withstanding battle, summoned at need and worn until the wave is repelled.

When a student activates their Hemoanima, their everyday clothes give way to a combat uniform unique to them — often a dark, militarized variant of a school uniform, a black gakuran or modified sailor fuku, marked with the same numeral as their armband. Alongside the armor comes their personal armament: a great scythe for the unit's scythe-wielding strategist, a heavy axe for its leader, a fan of floating knives for one fighter, even a piloted mecha for another. The forms are as individual as the people who summon them.

The transformation also brings out the Hemoanima marks more fully — in at least one case, ordinary makeup giving way to mascara that runs blood-red. Class Armor is not a costume but a fighting state: in it, the sickly can hold a weapon they could never lift unaided, the timid can stand their ground, and the merciless can tear an Invader apart. It is the form in which the line gets held — and the form in which the unit pays Hemoanima's price.

9.content:
Behind the waves of School Invaders stand the enemy commanders — the leadership directing the assault on the Last Defense Academy. Their existence is the clearest sign that the war is not a natural disaster or a mindless swarm, but a campaign with intent behind it.

Where the rank-and-file Invaders come in numbers to be cut down, the commanders are something more: stronger, deliberate, and capable of strategy. They appear at key points in the hundred-day siege, raising the stakes of a battle far beyond an ordinary wave and forcing the Special Defense Unit to fight at the very limits of their Hemoanima. Some days bring a commander to the field directly, and those are the days the unit remembers.

To the students, the commanders are a mystery wrapped around a threat. Their presence implies an organization, a hierarchy, and a purpose to the invasion — that someone, or something, wants the academy taken and is willing to spend Invaders by the wave to do it. Who commands the commanders, what they ultimately want, and how their existence connects to Sirei and the true nature of the war are among the questions the unit has not been able to answer. For now they are simply the hardest thing the line has to hold against.

10.content:
The Tokyo Residential Complex is the ordinary home world several members of the Special Defense Unit came from — the peaceful, unremarkable place where the attacks first began. For students like the unit's everyman protagonist, it represents everything the war took away.

Life there was mundane and repetitive, and that was exactly its charm: school, family dinners, lazy afternoons, childhood friends who let themselves into your house as if they owned it. It was the kind of place where the most pressing concern was a big test, and where "boring" was a quiet kind of happiness no one thought to be grateful for. Then the School Invaders came, tearing through the complex without warning, turning a normal day into the last normal day — and it was in that chaos that Sirei appeared and the unit was born.

For the students who lived there, the complex is now both a wound and an anchor. It is the home they were taken from, the people they left behind, and the reason many of them keep fighting at all — because somewhere back there are loved ones who might still be counting on them. More than one member of the unit measures the cost of the war against those "nothing days" they didn't appreciate until they were gone, and would give almost anything to have back.

11.content:
The chain that binds the Special Defense Unit to the Last Defense Academy is not made of locks but of love. When Sirei first assembled the students, it secured their obedience by threatening that the people they cared about — family, childhood friends, anyone they left behind — would be hurt or killed if they refused to fight.

It is a coercion that works precisely because it cannot be safely tested. Some students suspect the threat may be a bluff. But "probably a bluff" is not good enough when the stakes are a person you love: the protagonist himself reasons that he would rather be fooled than be the reason someone got hurt, and so he chooses to act on the threat as if it were real rather than gamble with a life. That logic — that the smallest chance of losing someone is unacceptable — is how Sirei keeps even its most skeptical conscripts in the fight.

For each member of the unit, the threat lands somewhere specific: a childhood friend, a parent, a sibling. It is the reason the self-doubting keep showing up, the reason even the self-serving and the reluctant stay, and a constant low hum beneath every battle. Whether Sirei could ever make good on the threat — and whether it ever intended to — is one of the cruelest open questions of the hundred days.

12.content:
Between the waves of School Invaders, the Last Defense Academy goes quiet, and the Special Defense Unit gets the strange task of simply living. Daily life on a rest day is the war's negative space — the hours that aren't spent fighting, where the students recover, bond, clash, and remember they are still teenagers.

Meals are taken together in the dining hall, and they matter more than food should: the table is where the unit's mismatched personalities collide and slowly cohere. There is downtime in the dormitory halls and common rooms — reading, dozing, training, tinkering, picking fights and patching them up. The unit's young detective buries herself in mystery novels; her brother makes a loud, embarrassing show of his devotion to her; the strategist talks tactics that bleed into something closer to friendship; the manic one performs for whoever will watch; the coward looks out for himself and is, somehow, the life of the party anyway.

These are also the hours when secrets surface and trust is built or broken — when someone admits they have no idea what they're doing, when an outsider is decided to be safe, when a wary, hardened group of strangers becomes, against the odds, something like comrades. Rest days are precious precisely because they are borrowed time: the countdown does not stop, the next wave is always coming, and everyone knows it. So they eat, and they talk, and they hold onto the quiet while it lasts.

13.content:
Beyond the shared Hemoanima they all wield, some members of the Special Defense Unit manifest unusual abilities of their own — gifts and quirks that set them apart even among an already extraordinary group.

The clearest example is the unit's youngest girl, a dreamy aspiring detective who carries an involuntary clairvoyance. It surfaces as sudden visions — fragments of the future, or hidden truths that someone has tried to keep buried — usually triggered by intense emotion or danger, and never quite when she wants it. The visions come unbidden, sometimes startling her or the people around her; there are no ill effects, only the frustration of a gift that refuses to obey her in the exact moments she needs it most. She cannot summon it on command, and it is as likely to leave her shaken and uncertain ("I saw — I think I saw...") as to hand her an answer.

Others in the unit have their own edges that blur the line between talent and something stranger — remarkable senses, instincts honed past the ordinary, skills that should not work as well as they do. These abilities are vital assets in a war fought at the limits of what teenagers can do, but they are unruly and personal, bound up with who each student is. They are not interchangeable tools so much as parts of the people who carry them — and, in more than one case, clues to mysteries the unit has not yet solved.

14.content:
Of all the questions hanging over the Last Defense Academy, the one the Special Defense Unit returns to most is the simplest and the hardest: what *is* Sirei?

It presents as a mascot — cute, cartoonish, harmless-looking — and yet it appeared at the exact moment the School Invaders first attacked, knew to find each student, and had a hundred-day war already prepared. It speaks through every monitor in the academy. It tracks a countdown whose ending it will not explain. And its eyes — those lens-like, mechanically moving things — suggest something built, something watching, something recording, beneath the friendly shell. The students who dare to look closely sense there is more inside than a mascot should hold.

The unit cannot decide what to make of it. Is Sirei their warden or their protector? Did it cause the war, or merely arrive to manage it? Does it know the answers it withholds, or is it as bound to this place as they are? It made each of them an offer and a threat, and then asked them to trust it with their lives — without ever earning that trust or explaining itself.

Whatever Sirei truly is, the answer seems tangled up with the origin of the Invaders, the purpose of the academy, and the meaning of the hundredth day. The students are, in their quieter moments, trying to work it out — watching, collecting clues, withholding their own conclusions. None of them has the full picture yet.

15.comment: What Waits at Day One Hundred (Hidden Truth)
15.content:
Sirei set the terms plainly enough: hold the Last Defense Academy for one hundred days. What it has never explained is what happens when that hundredth day arrives — and that silence is, quietly, the heaviest thing the Special Defense Unit carries.

The countdown is supposed to be a finish line. Survive the waves, repel the School Invaders, last the full hundred, and then — what? Sirei has promised an end, but not its shape. The students fill the blank with their own hopes and dreads: that reaching day one hundred means going home, that it means the war is finally over, that the people they were threatened with will be safe at last. Others, more wary, suspect the number is not a finish line at all but a threshold — that day one hundred is less an ending than a door, and that whatever lies on the other side may be the thing the war was always building toward.

There are too many unanswered questions for comfort. Why one hundred days, exactly? Why these particular students? Why this school? The enemy commanders imply a purpose; Sirei implies a plan; and the students cannot shake the feeling that they are pieces in something larger than the siege they can see. They keep fighting because the alternative was made unthinkable — and because, on the far side of one hundred days, there might be an answer worth surviving for. Or there might be the next thing entirely.

16.content:
For all that the Special Defense Unit fights with conviction, almost none of them fully believes the war is as simple as Sirei has framed it. Beneath the routine of battle days and rest days runs a current of unease — a sense, shared but rarely spoken aloud, that they are not being told the whole story.

The questions accumulate. Where did the School Invaders truly come from, and why did they begin in ordinary places like the Tokyo Residential Complex? Why were these specific teenagers — an everyman, an assassin, a detective's daughter, a sickly strategist, a delinquent, a coward, a pair of orphaned twins — chosen for Hemoanima and brought together here? Why does at least one of their own, the kindhearted girl beside the protagonist, carry such an unmistakable air of holding something back? What is built into Sirei's eyes? What did the coercion truly mean — and could it ever have been made good?

Some students chase these threads actively. The unit's young detective collects details the way her late father taught her to, certain that nothing is ever truly impossible — there is always a thread, if you find where it's tucked away. Others would rather not look too hard, afraid of what the answers might cost. But the questions do not go away, and the longer the siege runs, the louder they get. The students are, in their own ways, conducting an investigation no one assigned them: into the war, into Sirei, and into the truth of why any of this is happening at all.
